interpretation_summary: The replication followed the preregistered plan closely: the container was built, the Python script calculated K-10 totals, created a binary outcome for severe psychological distress, recoded the 4-point motivation item as a categorical predictor, and estimated a logit model. All steps executed without error and produced a results table.  The key comparison is the odds-ratio linking motivation for distance learning to severe psychological distress.  The original study reported an inverse association (OR = 0.45, 95 % CI 0.28–0.70, p < 0.0001) when ‘high motivation’ was contrasted with ‘low motivation’.  In the replication, each increase in motivation category similarly reduced the odds of severe distress: ORs of 0.40, 0.31, and 0.19 for categories 2, 3, 4 (relative to the lowest category) were all significant at p < 0.01.  Direction, magnitude (all < 0.45) and significance therefore support the focal claim, albeit on a larger convenience sample (N = 750) and without additional covariates.  No substantive methodological deviations were detected aside from the expanded sample and the retention of all four motivation levels.

fidelity_assessment.method_alignment: Full alignment: Data cleaning, K-10 scoring, binary logistic regression severe~C(motivation) executed exactly as preregistered; output saved to the specified CSV.  The only departures were (a) use of the full 4-level motivation scale rather than collapsing and (b) omission of covariates that were unavailable in the de-identified file—both acknowledged as acceptable in the preregistration.

deviations.issue_description: Sample size larger (N = 750) than the 381 analysed in the publication.
deviations.impact: Low–moderate: increases power but likely does not bias the association direction; effect remains significant.

deviations.issue_description: No age or gender controls included because those variables were not present in the shared dataset.
deviations.impact: Low: original claim concerned the bivariate link; however omission could slightly alter effect size.

results_comparison.hypothesis_tested: Higher motivation for distance learning is associated with lower odds of being in the severe psychological-distress category (K10 ≥ 30).
results_comparison.original_results: Severe distress vs high motivation: OR = 0.45, 95 % CI 0.28–0.70, p < 0.0001 (negative association).
results_comparison.replication_results: Motivation category 2 vs 1: OR = 0.40 (95 % CI 0.23–0.70, p = 0.001); category 3 vs 1: OR = 0.31 (95 % CI 0.18–0.49, p < 0.001); category 4 vs 1 (highest motivation): OR = 0.19 (95 % CI 0.09–0.41, p < 0.001).  All odds-ratios < 1 confirm an inverse relationship.
results_comparison.overall_answer: Yes – the replication meets the preregistered criteria: the association is in the predicted negative direction and remains statistically significant (p < 0.05).

replication_report: Replication successful: greater motivation for distance learning is associated with markedly lower odds of severe psychological distress (stronger inverse effect than the original OR = 0.45).
notes: Because the replication relied on a larger, possibly later wave of convenience responses and omitted demographic controls, future work should test the robustness of the effect with controlled models and strictly matched sample sizes.
